summary: Turns on the LLTD Responder driver through the official Machine policy path. Microsoft directly documents the same policy key, value name, and 1/0 semantics that the app uses for this LLTD Responder setting.

setting.name: LLTD Responder Driver Policy
setting.casual_explanation: The LLTD Responder lets other PCs discover this device on the local network during Windows network mapping and related discovery tasks.
setting.why_people_change_it: Admins change this when they need a PC to participate in old-style local network discovery or when they are troubleshooting why a device is not showing up in Windows network maps.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: This can help local discovery scenarios, but most people do not need to force it on and many modern home networks work fine without touching it.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents EnableRspndr under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD. As with the LLTDIO policy, a disabled or not configured state means default behavior applies rather than a published hard feature-off state.

allowed_values.label: Default behavior
allowed_values.meaning: Windows uses the default LLTD Responder behavior because the policy is not configured.

allowed_values.label: Policy disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The administrative template policy is disabled, which falls back to default LLTD Responder behavior.

allowed_values.label: Policy enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The LLTD Responder is explicitly turned on by policy.

targets.notes: Additional allow and prohibit booleans exist when the policy is enabled.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app writes EnableRspndr = 1, which matches the documented policy enable state.

windows_defaults.label: Policy not configured

states.rationale: Microsoft says default Responder behavior applies when the policy is not configured.

windows_defaults.notes: Disabled policy also returns to default behavior.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Devices that do not need forced local discovery participation
avoid_for: Admins who intentionally want the responder forced on

states.rationale: Do not force the policy unless you specifically need the responder on.

recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are trying to enforce local discovery participation.

recommended_profiles.label: Force enable responder
intended_for: Managed devices that should answer LLTD discovery requests
avoid_for: People expecting a generic speed tweak

states.rationale: This is the Microsoft-documented policy enable state.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You need the device to participate in LLTD discovery.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You do not have a concrete network discovery reason for changing it.
tradeoffs: The device becomes easier to discover through LLTD-aware workflows.
This is a specialized discovery setting, not a universal optimization.

evidence.title: Microsoft administrative template for LLTD Responder
evidence.summary: The LinkLayerTopologyDiscovery administrative template documents EnableRspndr at HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD with enabled value 1 and disabled value 0.

evidence.title: Microsoft help text for LLTD Responder policy
evidence.summary: Microsoft explains that enabling the policy changes Responder behavior and that disabling or not configuring the policy leaves the default behavior in place.

evidence.title: Current network tweak provider
evidence.summary: The current app writes EnableRspndr = 1 for this tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD -> EnableRspndr in the LinkLayerTopologyDiscovery administrative template, with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. The companion ADML help text explains that enabling the policy turns on Responder behavior and disabling or not configuring it leaves default behavior in place.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="LLTD_EnableRspndr" class="Machine" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LLTD" valueName="EnableRspndr"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> ... <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>; LinkLayerTopologyDiscovery.adml: If you disable or do not configure this policy setting, the default behavior for the Responder will apply.
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft administrative template defines the exact LLTD responder policy path and value name used by the app, and the companion ADML help text documents the enable/default behavior.
